title: Ransomware in 2026: When the Encryption Stops but the Extortion Doesn't
excerpt: Ransomware crews are dropping encryption for pure data-theft extortion. Here is how the 2026 playbook really works and the defenses that actually blunt it.
bodyHtml:
For two decades the word ransomware conjured a single image: a locked screen, a countdown timer, and a demand for cryptocurrency in exchange for a decryption key. That mental model is now dangerously out of date. The defining shift of 2026 is that a growing share of "ransomware" incidents involve no encryption at all. Attackers steal the data, threaten to publish it, and skip the noisy, error-prone step of scrambling files entirely. The lock is gone; the leverage remains.

This is not a cosmetic change. It rewrites the economics of attack and defense, and it quietly invalidates the one control most organizations relied on as their safety net: backups. If the crew never encrypted anything, a clean restore does nothing to stop the threat of public disclosure.

Why crews are abandoning the lock

Encryption was always the weakest link in the operation. It is slow, it trips endpoint detection as files change en masse, and it forces the attacker to manage key infrastructure and a working decryptor. Pure exfiltration sidesteps all of that. The smarter operators realized that the encryption step was a liability, not the product. The product was always the leverage, and leverage comes from holding sensitive data hostage.

Three forces pushed this evolution forward:

  • Backups got better. Immutable, offline, and well-tested backups made encryption-only attacks survivable, so attackers needed a threat that backups could not answer.
  • Data lives in the cloud now. A single misconfigured object store or over-permissioned SaaS token can yield millions of records through legitimate API calls, with no malware ever touching a local disk.
  • Regulation became the weapon. Breach-notification regimes mean a confirmed data theft triggers mandatory disclosure regardless of whether systems are restored, so the reputational and compliance damage is baked in the moment data leaves the building.
Data exfiltration is now the primary extortion lever. Compliance preparedness has become as critical to ransomware resilience as backup infrastructure ever was.

The industrialized RaaS supply chain

None of this scales without a business model, and ransomware-as-a-service has matured into exactly that. A small core team builds and maintains the tooling, the leak site, and the negotiation infrastructure, then leases it to affiliates who carry out intrusions. Revenue-split arrangements between operators and affiliates function like commission structures, and the more aggressive programs court experienced affiliates with more generous splits to poach talent from rivals.

The practical consequence for defenders is that you are rarely facing a lone genius. You are facing a division of labor: initial-access brokers who sell footholds, affiliates who handle lateral movement and theft, and a platform team that handles the extortion mechanics. Each layer is replaceable, which makes the ecosystem resilient to takedowns and easy to re-enter. When law enforcement disrupts one brand, the operators and affiliates simply regroup under a new name, carrying their tooling and relationships with them.

This specialization also explains why the barrier to entry has effectively collapsed. An aspiring criminal no longer needs to write malware, build leak-site infrastructure, or learn to negotiate. They can rent all of it. That accessibility is precisely why the volume of activity has settled into an elevated new normal rather than spiking and fading like earlier malware waves. The infrastructure is permanent, professionalized, and constantly recruiting.

Living off the land

Modern intrusions increasingly avoid custom malware in favor of tools already present on the target. Remote administration utilities, native scripting, and legitimate file-transfer software let attackers blend into normal operations and move laterally without tripping signature-based controls. A large share of intrusions now lean on everyday administrative software that no antivirus would ever flag, because the software is genuinely legitimate and used by real administrators every day. When the toolkit is indistinguishable from a busy IT department's daily work, detection has to shift from "what binary is this" to "is this behavior normal for this account, on this host, at this hour."

This evasion strategy is the connective tissue between initial access and exfiltration. An attacker who lands quietly, moves laterally using built-in tooling, and stages data using a sanctioned file-transfer utility can spend days inside a network before anyone notices. The dwell time is where the damage is done, and reducing it is one of the highest-leverage investments a defender can make. Every hour an intruder operates undetected is another batch of records walking out the door.

What actually moves the needle

Because the threat model has shifted from availability to confidentiality, defense has to shift with it. A backup-first strategy is necessary but no longer sufficient. The goal is to make data theft harder to accomplish quietly and to detect bulk movement before it completes.

  • Treat exfiltration as the primary event. Instrument egress. Alert on large or unusual outbound transfers, anomalous cloud API calls, and access to data stores from new identities or locations.
  • Shrink the blast radius of identity. Enforce least privilege on human and machine accounts, scope SaaS and cloud tokens tightly, and rotate them aggressively.
  • Segment ruthlessly. Flat networks let one foothold reach everything. Segmentation slows lateral movement and buys detection time.
  • Rehearse the disclosure decision. Build the legal, communications, and regulatory playbook before an incident, because the timeline for notification starts the moment theft is confirmed.
  • Hunt for living-off-the-land behavior. Baseline normal administrative activity so abnormal use of legitimate tools stands out.

The mindset shift defenders need

The hardest part of adapting is psychological. Security teams spent years optimizing for recovery: snapshots, runbooks, restore drills. Those investments still matter for the encryption-based attacks that have not disappeared. But the question that now keeps incident responders awake is no longer "can we restore" but "what left the building, and can we prove it didn't."

That reframing is uncomfortable because confidentiality failures are harder to undo than availability failures. You cannot un-leak a database. The only durable defense is to make exfiltration loud, slow, and limited, so that even a successful intrusion does not automatically become a catastrophic disclosure. In 2026, the organizations that fare best are the ones that stopped treating ransomware as an IT recovery problem and started treating it as a data-governance problem with a deadline attached.

title: Poisoning the Well: How Supply-Chain Attacks Are Hitting Developers Where They Build
excerpt: Dependency confusion, typosquatting, and maintainer takeovers turned package registries into a battlefield. A defensive breakdown of the 2026 supply chain.
bodyHtml:
The most efficient way to compromise a thousand organizations in 2026 is not to attack a thousand organizations. It is to attack one package that all thousand of them install. Software supply-chain attacks have moved from a theoretical worry to a routine event, and the pattern is consistent: poison something developers trust, and let their own build pipelines distribute the payload for you.

What makes this class of attack so dangerous is that it inverts the usual trust relationship. Developers have been trained to pull dependencies freely, automate installs, and trust the registry. Attackers have noticed, and they are systematically abusing every assumption baked into modern package management.

The four doors into your dependency tree

Supply-chain compromises in 2026 rarely rely on a single trick. The notable campaigns combine several techniques so that closing one door does not stop the intrusion. Understanding the doors individually is the first step to closing all of them.

  • Dependency confusion. An attacker publishes a public package whose name matches a company's private, internal package. Misconfigured tooling pulls the public impostor instead of the internal original, executing attacker code inside a trusted environment.
  • Typosquatting. A package is published under a name one keystroke away from a popular library. A single typo in a manifest or a copy-paste error pulls the malicious twin.
  • Maintainer account takeover. Attackers phish or credential-stuff their way into a legitimate maintainer's account and push a malicious version of a genuinely popular package, inheriting all of its trust and download volume.
  • Wormable propagation. The newest twist: payloads that prioritize stealing publishing tokens and source-control access tokens, then use them to automatically infect and republish other packages, turning a single compromise into a self-spreading outbreak.
The distinguishing feature of recent campaigns is not any single technique but the use of multiple attack vectors working in concert, so defenders who patch one path are still exposed through another.

The install script is the soft underbelly

Much of the damage in registry attacks happens before a single line of the imported code is ever called by your application. Lifecycle and install scripts run automatically the moment a package is fetched, which means a malicious dependency can profile the developer's machine, harvest environment variables, and exfiltrate secrets during installation alone. This is why the broader ecosystem is moving toward blocking install scripts by default. If a package can execute arbitrary code simply by being downloaded, then the moment of installation is the moment of compromise.

The blast radius is widened further by where developers keep their secrets. Continuous integration runners and developer laptops are saturated with cloud credentials, registry tokens, and source-control access tokens. A package that runs during install in that environment is sitting on a treasure chest, and the most damaging payloads are built specifically to find and exfiltrate those keys.

There is a second, subtler danger in the install phase: environment profiling. Some malicious packages do not immediately fire their payload. Instead they quietly fingerprint the machine they landed on, checking whether they are in a sandbox, a researcher's analysis box, or a genuine corporate developer environment worth attacking. Only the high-value environments get the real payload, which makes these packages harder to catch in automated scanning and lets them dwell in the ecosystem longer before discovery. The attacker is being patient and selective, treating each install as reconnaissance before committing to the theft.

Building a defensible pipeline

The good news is that supply-chain risk is one of the more tractable problems in security, because it is largely a matter of discipline and tooling rather than chasing a determined human adversary in real time. The practices below compound: each one is useful alone, and together they make a quiet compromise far harder to pull off.

  • Pin and hash dependencies. Lock exact versions and verify cryptographic integrity at build time so a republished malicious version cannot silently slip in.
  • Maintain a software bill of materials. A live inventory of every component lets you answer "are we affected" in minutes rather than days when a compromise is disclosed.
  • Disable install scripts by default. Run them only for packages that genuinely need them, and audit those exceptions.
  • Sign and attest your builds. Keyless signing tied to your build workflow's identity, plus provenance attestations, create a verifiable record of what was built, from what source, by which pipeline.
  • Scope and rotate tokens. Treat publishing and source-control tokens as crown jewels. Limit their permissions, shorten their lifetimes, and rotate them on a schedule.
  • Reserve your namespaces. Register your internal package names publicly to neutralize dependency-confusion attempts before they start.

From static inventory to continuous governance

The maturity curve for supply-chain security runs from "we have no idea what we depend on" to "we generate, sign, and verify an inventory on every build, and we can prove the provenance of every artifact we ship." Most organizations are somewhere in the messy middle, and the gap is where attackers live.

The strategic lesson of 2026 is that your dependency tree is part of your attack surface, whether or not you treat it that way. Every package you install is code you are choosing to run with the privileges of your build system. The registries are not going to solve this for you, though their default-secure changes help. The teams that stay out of the headlines are the ones that decided their build pipeline deserves the same scrutiny as their production network, and instrumented it accordingly. Trust in open source is not the problem; unverified trust is. The fix is to keep the trust and add the verification.

title: Identity Is the New Perimeter: Inside the Rise of Session and Token Theft
excerpt: Attackers stopped cracking passwords and started stealing sessions. How infostealers and token theft sail past MFA, and how defenders fight back in 2026.
bodyHtml:
The cleanest way to break into a modern enterprise in 2026 is not to break in at all. It is to log in. As organizations moved their crown jewels into cloud applications, the network perimeter dissolved and a new one took its place: identity. Attackers followed the value, and the result is a threat landscape where stolen sessions and authentication tokens, not cracked passwords, are the currency of compromise.

This shift has a counterintuitive consequence. Many organizations rolled out multi-factor authentication, declared the password problem solved, and moved on. Attackers responded by simply going around MFA rather than through it. The lock on the front door got stronger, so they stopped using the front door.

Why passwords stopped mattering

Stealing a password used to be the whole game. Now it is almost an afterthought, because what attackers really want is a valid session. When you authenticate to an application, the server hands your browser a session cookie or token that says "this person already proved who they are." That token grants access until it expires or is revoked, and crucially, it represents an already-completed MFA challenge. Steal the token and you inherit the authenticated session without ever facing the second factor.

This is why infostealer malware has become such a force. Rather than logging keystrokes for passwords, modern stealers harvest session cookies, saved credentials, and cloud tokens directly from the endpoint, then ship them to the attacker. The criminal loads the cookies into their own browser and walks straight into every application those cookies are valid for.

When the token already encodes a completed MFA challenge, stealing the token is a way of stealing the authentication itself. The second factor was never bypassed, because it was never confronted.

The MFA-bypass toolkit

Token theft via malware is only one path. The broader category of identity attacks has produced a whole toolkit for getting past second factors, and the techniques are notable precisely because they target the human and procedural seams around MFA rather than the cryptography itself.

  • Adversary-in-the-middle phishing. A proxy page sits between the victim and the real login, relaying the MFA challenge in real time and capturing the resulting session token. The victim authenticates successfully; the attacker keeps the session.
  • Help-desk social engineering. Attackers call IT support impersonating an employee and talk their way into a credential or MFA reset, frequently using voice-based pretexting over collaboration platforms.
  • MFA fatigue. A flood of push notifications wears the target down until they approve one just to stop the noise, which is why number-matching and push-only flows are increasingly seen as liabilities.
  • OAuth and token abuse. A malicious or over-permissioned application is granted access, giving the attacker persistent, token-backed entry that survives password changes and does not look like a login at all.

The cookie is the new password

The strategic insight for defenders is that session artifacts have become the asset worth protecting, and they are far harder to manage than passwords. A password can be changed. A leaked session cookie is valid until it expires or is explicitly revoked, and most organizations have poor visibility into where their live sessions even are. The volume of stolen cookies, cloud tokens, and machine credentials circulating in criminal markets dwarfs the value of plaintext passwords precisely because tokens skip the authentication step entirely.

Server-side evasion

The arms race continues to escalate. Newer stealers have moved toward decrypting harvested credentials on attacker infrastructure rather than on the victim's device, specifically to avoid the endpoint security tooling built to catch on-device credential theft. The endpoint only ever sees an encrypted blob being copied, which looks far less suspicious than a process prying open the browser's credential vault. The lesson is that endpoint detection alone cannot be the whole answer when adversaries deliberately move the sensitive operations off the endpoint.

This evasion mirrors a broader pattern across identity attacks: adversaries study the exact place defenders are watching and then conduct the sensitive step somewhere else. Watching the endpoint? Decrypt server-side. Watching the login? Steal the post-login token. Watching for new passwords? Abuse an OAuth grant that never needs one. Each move is a deliberate sidestep around a specific control, which is why layered, assume-breach defense matters so much more than any single tool.

Defending the new perimeter

If identity is the perimeter, then identity needs the kind of layered, assume-breach defense that networks used to get. No single control is sufficient, but together they raise the cost of a stolen session dramatically.

  • Adopt phishing-resistant authentication. Hardware-backed and platform passkeys bind authentication cryptographically to the legitimate domain, so a proxy phishing page simply cannot complete the flow.
  • Bind sessions to context. Tie tokens to device posture, network, and behavioral signals so that replaying a cookie from a new device or location triggers re-authentication or revocation.
  • Shorten session lifetimes and revoke aggressively. A stolen token is only as valuable as the window it stays valid.
  • Govern OAuth grants. Inventory third-party application access, enforce least privilege on scopes, and review consented integrations regularly.
  • Harden the help desk. Require strong identity verification before any credential or MFA reset, and treat reset requests as high-risk events.
  • Monitor for impossible sessions. Alert on concurrent logins from distant locations, sudden device changes, and token use that does not match the user's pattern.

The organizations that adapt fastest are the ones that internalized a simple reframing: in 2026, authentication is not a one-time gate at login but a continuous judgment about whether the entity holding this session still deserves it. The perimeter moved. Defense has to move with it.

title: The AI-fication of Cyberthreats: Deepfakes, Prompt Injection, and Rogue Agents
excerpt: Generative AI handed attackers scale and polish, while AI agents opened a new attack surface. A defender's guide to the AI-driven threats defining 2026.
bodyHtml:
Artificial intelligence did not invent a new category of attack so much as it removed the friction from the old ones. The phishing email that used to betray itself with broken grammar now reads like it came from your CFO. The reconnaissance that took a skilled operator days now happens at machine speed. And as organizations rush to deploy their own AI agents, attackers have discovered an entirely new surface to subvert. This is the AI-fication of cyberthreats, and in 2026 it is reshaping both how attacks are launched and what attackers aim at.

The honest framing matters here. AI has not produced a wave of unstoppable super-weapons. What it has produced is a force multiplier that makes mediocre attackers competent and competent attackers terrifyingly efficient. The defensive challenge is one of scale and authenticity, not of some unprecedented technical wizardry.

Social engineering at industrial scale

The first and most mature impact is on social engineering. Generative models can produce fluent, context-aware, personalized lures in any language and at effectively unlimited volume. The tells defenders trained users to spot, awkward phrasing, generic greetings, are gone. Worse, AI-driven reconnaissance can map an organization and tailor each message to the recipient's role and relationships.

Synthetic media takes this further. Voice and video deepfakes now make it plausible for an employee to receive a video call from someone who looks and sounds like a senior executive, authorizing an urgent transfer or a credential reset. The attack does not need to be perfect; it needs to be good enough to survive a few seconds of a stressed employee's scrutiny.

The danger of AI-generated social engineering is not that any single message is undetectable. It is that defenders must now be right every time across a volume of attacks no human team was ever sized to review.

The economics are lopsided. An attacker can generate and test thousands of variations of a lure for almost nothing, keeping whatever survives and discarding the rest, while every defender and employee on the receiving end must evaluate each attempt with finite attention. That asymmetry is the real story of AI-assisted social engineering, and it is why control cannot rest on human vigilance alone.

Prompt injection: the new injection flaw

The second front is entirely new, and it belongs to the organizations deploying AI rather than the ones being phished. Large language models cannot reliably distinguish between the instructions their developers gave them and the untrusted content they are asked to process. Feed a model a document, a web page, or an email that contains hidden instructions, and it may follow them. This is prompt injection, and it has rapidly become one of the fastest-growing attack categories because it targets a structural weakness in how these systems work.

Prompt injection is dangerous in proportion to what the model can do. A chatbot that only answers questions is a limited target. A model wired into your email, your databases, and your internal tools is a different story entirely, because a successful injection can turn the model into a confused deputy that exfiltrates data or takes actions on the attacker's behalf using the application's own privileges.

The agentic escalation

The risk compounds with agentic AI: autonomous systems that plan and execute multi-step tasks with minimal human oversight. An agent is essentially a model that has been handed the keys to do things, not just say things. That makes a compromised agent meaningfully worse than a compromised human, because it can be made to operate at scale, conducting many simultaneous, individually-tailored manipulations, and it executes its instructions without the hesitation or conscience that sometimes stops a person mid-mistake.

  • Confused-deputy actions. An injected agent uses its legitimate access to read, move, or delete data it should not touch.
  • Privilege chaining. An agent with access to multiple tools can string them together in ways its designers never anticipated.
  • Scaled deception. One compromised agent can run a thousand tailored conversations at once, each engineered to maximize success.

Defending in the age of AI threats

The defensive response splits cleanly along the two fronts. For AI-powered social engineering, the answer is less about detecting the perfect fake and more about removing the human's ability to be the single point of failure.

  • Verify out of band. Require a second, independent channel to confirm high-risk requests like fund transfers and credential resets, regardless of how convincing the original looks.
  • Adopt phishing-resistant authentication. Cryptographic, domain-bound credentials neutralize even a flawless phishing lure because the credential refuses to work on the wrong site.
  • Train for the new reality. Retire awareness programs built around spelling mistakes and teach verification habits instead.

For AI you deploy, treat the model as untrusted by default:

  • Constrain privileges. Give agents the minimum access required, and require human approval for consequential actions.
  • Isolate untrusted input. Assume any content the model ingests may carry hostile instructions, and architect around that assumption.
  • Log and monitor agent behavior. Treat an agent's actions as an auditable event stream, and alert on anomalies just as you would for a privileged account.

The asymmetry, and how to close it

AI has handed attackers leverage on both offense and reconnaissance, and defenders feel the asymmetry acutely. But the same tools cut both ways. AI-assisted detection can triage alerts, surface anomalies, and summarize incidents at the speed the threats now demand. The organizations that struggle in 2026 are those that deployed AI capabilities faster than they deployed the controls to govern them, opening new attack surfaces while leaving social-engineering defenses anchored in a pre-AI world. The ones that thrive treat AI as a domain that needs the same security discipline as any other powerful technology: least privilege, untrusted input handling, verification, and monitoring. The fundamentals did not change. The stakes and the speed did.

title: Edge Under Siege: Why Your VPN and Firewall Became the Front Line
excerpt: Attackers shifted to the network edge, hammering VPNs, firewalls, and gateways with zero-days and aging n-days alike. Inside the 2026 perimeter siege.
bodyHtml:
For years the devices guarding the network edge were treated as solved problems: install the appliance, point traffic through it, and trust it to do its job. In 2026 that complacency has become one of the most reliable ways into an enterprise. VPN gateways, firewalls, secure-access appliances, and identity systems, the very products sold to keep attackers out, have become the perimeter's softest target. The edge is under siege, and the trend line is steep.

The logic from the attacker's side is brutally simple. These devices sit directly on the internet by design, they are trusted to broker access to everything behind them, and they are notoriously hard to inspect with the endpoint tooling that protects ordinary servers and laptops. Compromise one and you get a foothold that is both privileged and invisible to most defensive telemetry.

The shift to the perimeter

The data tells a stark story: exploitation of edge and network devices has surged from a small fraction of vulnerability-driven breaches to a substantial share in the span of a single year. A growing majority of zero-days aimed at enterprises now target infrastructure rather than user software, with VPN appliances, firewalls, and identity platforms leading the list. The attackers making this pivot are not exclusively elite nation-states, though such actors are heavily represented; the techniques have diffused widely enough that the perimeter is contested ground for the whole spectrum of adversaries.

Why this category specifically? Edge devices combine three properties that make them irresistible:

  • Internet exposure by design. They must be reachable from outside, so there is no "move them off the public network" mitigation.
  • High trust, low visibility. They broker access to the internal network but often run closed firmware that resists installing security agents or doing forensic analysis.
  • Direct path to access. A successful exploit frequently hands the attacker authenticated network access immediately, skipping the slow grind of phishing and lateral movement.
Edge appliances are the rare target that is simultaneously exposed to everyone, trusted by everything behind it, and difficult for defenders to see inside. That combination is why the perimeter became the front line.

The collapsing exploitation window

Speed is the other half of the story. The comfortable assumption that organizations have weeks to patch after a vulnerability is disclosed no longer holds at the edge. The window between public disclosure and active mass exploitation has compressed dramatically, in many cases to a matter of days. Once a flaw in a widely deployed appliance becomes public, automated scanning finds exposed instances almost immediately, and patching becomes a race the defender is already losing.

Just as troubling, attackers have not abandoned old vulnerabilities. A significant share of the most-targeted flaws are years old, sometimes a decade or more, lingering on unpatched perimeter devices that nobody got around to updating. These n-day vulnerabilities are arguably more dangerous than zero-days because the fix already exists and is simply not applied. The attacker does not even need novel research, just a scanner and a list of organizations that fell behind.

Why patching is harder here

Edge devices are uniquely painful to patch. They are critical-path infrastructure, so updating them means scheduled downtime that business owners resist. They often run vendor firmware on fixed release cycles, which means the fix may not even be available the instant a flaw is disclosed. And many organizations simply lose track of every appliance they own, leaving forgotten devices exposed for years. The result is a target-rich environment of known-vulnerable, internet-facing hardware.

There is also a forensic blind spot that compounds the problem. Because these appliances rarely allow installing security agents or extracting detailed logs, an organization that suspects a device was compromised often cannot confirm it, scope it, or fully clean it. The safe assumption after a perimeter-device incident is that any credential the device touched is now suspect, which turns a single appliance compromise into a sweeping and expensive cleanup. That uncertainty is itself a cost, and it is one of the strongest arguments for patching the edge before an incident rather than investigating after one.

Holding the line at the edge

Defending the perimeter in 2026 requires treating edge appliances as the high-risk, high-value targets they are rather than as set-and-forget infrastructure. The priorities are visibility, speed, and assuming the device can fall.

  • Know your edge. Maintain a complete, current inventory of every internet-facing appliance. You cannot defend what you have forgotten you own.
  • Patch the perimeter first. Treat edge-device advisories as emergencies with a same-day or next-day target, not part of the routine monthly cycle.
  • Reduce exposure. Restrict management interfaces to trusted networks, disable unused features and services, and minimize the attack surface each device presents.
  • Monitor for post-exploitation behavior. Since the devices themselves are hard to inspect, watch the traffic and authentication patterns around them for signs that one has been turned against you.
  • Assume compromise is possible. Segment what sits behind the edge so a breached appliance does not equal a breached enterprise, and plan for credential rotation if a device is suspected.
  • Subscribe to actionable intelligence. Track which vulnerabilities are being actively exploited so you can prioritize the flaws that matter now over the long tail that may never be touched.

The perimeter that fights back

There is a bitter irony in security appliances becoming the breach vector, and it carries a lesson worth internalizing. No device is inherently trustworthy because of the role it plays; trust has to be earned continuously through patching, monitoring, and the assumption that even the guard at the gate can be turned. The organizations that fare best in 2026 are the ones that stopped treating the edge as a fortified wall and started treating it as the most-attacked, least-visible part of their estate, deserving of the fastest patches, the tightest configuration, and the closest watch. The perimeter did not disappear. It became the battlefield.
